Data is collected from the garbage collector (GC).Memory usage keeps growing;You need to know who allocates too often or too much. #### Typical findingsTemporary objects inside loops;Unbounded caches or slices;Unreleased references keeping memory alive.go tool pprof http://localhost:6060/debug/pprof/heap AllocObjects / AllocSpace → total allocations;InUseObjects / InUseSpace → currently live objects.Use flags like or to switch views.
Goroutine Profile - snapshot of all goroutines Captures stack traces of all goroutines at a single point in time.The app freezes or stops responding;Goroutine count keeps increasing;You suspect a deadlock or goroutine leak. #### Typical findingsGoroutines stuck on with no receiver; never reaches zero;Infinite without a .curl http://localhost:6060/debug/pprof/goroutine?debug2 You'll see text stack traces. Look for "sleep", "chan receive", "mutex", etc. Usually it's easy to spot where execution is stuck.
Block Profile - where goroutines are waiting Tracks how long goroutines are blocked, waiting on synchronization primitives (channels, mutexes, conditions).The app hangs under low load;High latency in simple operations;You want to find wait points that slow down performance. #### Typical findingsShared data structures causing contention;Backpressure in worker queues.go tool pprof http://localhost:6060/debug/pprof/block
Mutex Profile - lock contention Records how long goroutines hold a mutex and how long others wait for it.CPU usage is low but app is slow;Throughput doesn't scale with concurrency;You suspect shared locks or global bottlenecks. #### Typical findingsA global in a hot path;Shared maps without sharding;Logging or metrics inside critical sections.go tool pprof http://localhost:6060/debug/pprof/mutex
Allocs Profile - all allocations (including freed ones) Shows all memory allocations, not just the ones still in use. Useful to understand allocation rate and GC pressure.The app allocates too frequently (high GC load);You're optimizing short-lived, high-throughput operations. #### Typical findingsRepeated string concatenations (, );Allocating new on each request;Inefficient append or map usage.go tool pprof http://localhost:6060/debug/pprof/allocs Block and Mutex profiling should not be enabled permanently in production. Use sampling values to reduce overhead, for example:runtime.SetBlockProfileRate(10000)runtime.SetMutexProfileFraction(100)Enable profiling on a running service:Run a go command to collect profile informationgo tool pprof http://localhost:6060/debug/pprof/profile?seconds30
Using pprof directly in code Recording a CPU profile to a filego tool pprof cpu.pprof pprof top pprof web
Best practices for production Restrict access to (e.g., Basic Auth, internal IPs, env flag).Don't run CPU profiling all the time - it adds ~5–10% overhead.CPU profiles should be at least 10–30 secondsHeap profiles are safe to collect in production.For visualization, use:
— quick online inspection;Parca — continuous profiling.
Reading profiling results
What a pprof graph represents generates a call graph, where:Edge (arrow) - a call from one function to another;Node weight - how much CPU time or memory that function consumed;Edge weight - how much cost was passed to the callee functions.In short, the graph shows who calls whom and where time or memory is being spent.Wide bottom block → hottest function dominating → too many allocations high → contentionMany narrow repeated blocks → allocations inside a loop
Common visualization modes pprof top Showing nodes accounting 85.73%, 34.29s of 40.00s total flat flat% % cum cum% 10.23s 25.6% 25.6% 18.92s 47.3% main.work 8.69s 21.7% 47.3% 10.35s 25.9% processData - time spent inside the function itself (excluding callees) cum (cumulative) - total time including callees / - relative to total runtime ##### Highlights:Large flat time - optimize that specific function.Large cum time but small flat - the problem is in a callee.Shows annotated source code lines:pprof list compute Total: 40s ROUTINE compute main.go 10.00s 15.00s flat, cum 37.50% of Total You can see which specific lines consume CPU or allocate memory — ideal for micro-optimizations.
web (or go tool pprof -http=:6060) Opens an interactive call graph in your browser. - functions consuming the most resources - time or memory weight - function call relationship ##### Highlights:The wider and redder the box, the hotter the function.A flamegraph is the most intuitive format.main └── handleRequest ├── parseJSON └── processData ├── validate └── saveToDB
Each rectangle = a function: = total time (width = cost) = call chain (from main to leaf functions) ##### Highlights:If is wide - JSON encoding is CPU-heavy.If dominates - DB operations are the bottleneck. ##### Example interpretation For example, your flamegraph shows: main → handle → json.Marshal → reflect.Value.Interface and takes 40% of CPU time. That's a clear indicator of slow reflection-based serialization - replace with faster encoder like jsoniter or easyjson.Start with the widest blocks at the bottom — they consume the most time or memory.Ignore runtime internals (, ) unless something abnormal shows up.Look for repeating narrow peaks — they often mean inefficient work inside loops.If CPU looks fine but app hangs, check block or mutex profiles — likely synchronization issues, not CPU load.go tool pprof -diff_base heap1.pprof heap2.pprof live objects currently in memory"memory leak" — but may just be a cache or bufferall allocations (even freed)engineers think "growth = leak" — it's not
Visualization tools comparison Quick inspection, remote serversImmediate hotspot identificationReal-time production monitoringProfiling is one of the most valuable tools for diagnosing performance issues in Go applications, and provides everything you need - from understanding CPU hotspots to uncovering memory leaks, goroutine leaks, synchronization bottlenecks, and inefficient allocation patterns.The key to using pprof effectively is knowing which profile to capture, how to interpret what you see, and how to compare snapshots over time. CPU profiles reveal hot paths, heap profiles uncover leaks or excessive allocations, goroutine dumps expose deadlocks or runaway concurrency, while block and mutex profiles highlight contention that's invisible to standard metrics.Always start from the widest blocks in flamegraphs.Use diffing to compare “before/after” optimizations.Enable advanced profiles (block/mutex) only when needed.Treat pprof as part of your standard debugging workflow - not as a last resort.Mastering turns performance debugging from guesswork into a repeatable, data-driven process. Once your team gets comfortable reading profiles, performance problems that used to take days can be solved in minutes.Measure, don't guess - profile first.

item.title: Build High-Performance gRPC Services in Golang: Connection Management and Streaming Guide
item.author: Aarav Joshi
item.content:encoded:
As a best-selling author, I invite you to explore my books on Amazon. Don't forget to follow me on Medium and show your support. Thank you! Your support means the world! When I first started working with distributed systems, I quickly realized that efficient communication between services is crucial. Traditional HTTP APIs often fall short in performance-critical applications. That's where gRPC comes in. gRPC uses Protocol Buffers for serialization and HTTP/2 for transport, making it ideal for microservices. In this article, I'll share how to build high-performance gRPC services in Golang, focusing on connection management and streaming. We'll dive into practical code examples and strategies I've used to reduce latency and handle high throughput.Let me start by explaining why gRPC is a game-changer. Unlike REST, which relies on JSON and HTTP/1.1, gRPC leverages binary protocols and multiplexing. This means multiple requests can share a single connection without blocking. I've seen services handle tens of thousands of requests per second with minimal overhead. The key is setting up the server and client correctly from the beginning.Here's a basic setup for a gRPC server in Golang. I'll build on this throughout the article.This code sets up a gRPC server with keep-alive settings and a connection pool. Keep-alive ensures idle connections don't time out unexpectedly. I've found this prevents many network-related issues in production.Connection pooling is vital for performance. Each new gRPC connection involves a TLS handshake and protocol negotiation, which adds latency. By reusing connections, we avoid this overhead. In one project, connection pooling cut average response times by 40% under load.Let's look at how the connection pool works.This function checks if a connection to the target exists. If not, it creates one. The read-write lock ensures thread safety. I use PermitWithoutStream: true to keep connections alive even when no streams are active. This is useful for services that communicate intermittently.Bidirectional streaming allows real-time data exchange. Imagine a chat application or stock ticker. Both client and server can send messages at any time. Handling this efficiently requires careful management of goroutines and channels.Here's a handler for bidirectional streams.This handler uses two goroutines: one for receiving messages and one for sending. The channel buffers messages to prevent blocking. Atomic counters track metrics without locks. In my tests, this setup handles thousands of concurrent streams smoothly.Compression is another area where gRPC shines. By default, Protocol Buffers are efficient, but we can add gzip compression for larger payloads. This reduces network usage without significant CPU cost.Here's how to enable compression on the client.On the server, you can set compression in the server options. I've used this to cut message sizes by 70% in data-intensive applications.Load balancing is essential for scaling. gRPC supports client-side load balancing out of the box. You can use DNS or manual configuration to distribute requests.Here's a simple client-side load balancer.This round-robin approach spreads load evenly. In production, you might use more sophisticated methods like least connections or health checks.Performance monitoring helps identify bottlenecks. I track active streams, message rates, and errors. This data informs scaling decisions.Here's how to expose metrics via HTTP.This endpoint provides real-time insights. I've integrated similar setups with Prometheus for alerting.Error handling is critical for reliability. gRPC connections can fail due to network issues. Implementing retries with backoff improves resilience.Here's a retry mechanism.This retries the RPC call up to three times with increasing delays. In practice, I combine this with circuit breakers to prevent cascading failures.Throughput optimization involves tuning several parameters. Setting appropriate message sizes and stream limits prevents memory exhaustion.Here's an example with custom options.I adjust these based on workload characteristics. For video streaming, larger messages make sense. For chat, smaller messages are better.In production, security is non-negotiable. Always use TLS for gRPC connections. Here's how to set it up.This encrypts all communication. I've seen this prevent data breaches in multi-tenant environments.Structured logging improves debuggability. I use JSON logging to track requests across services.This helps correlate logs in distributed tracing systems.Resource management is crucial. Goroutines can leak if not managed properly. Always use context for cancellation.This ensures resources are freed when clients disconnect.Testing is a key part of development. I write unit tests for all components.Automated tests catch regressions early.Deployment strategies include blue-green deployments and canary releases. gRPC's health checking support facilitates this.Here's a health check implementation.Load balancers use this to route traffic only to healthy instances.In conclusion, building high-performance gRPC services in Golang requires attention to connection management, streaming, and optimization. By reusing connections, handling streams efficiently, and monitoring performance, you can achieve low latency and high throughput. The code examples I've shared are from real-world applications. They've helped me scale services to millions of requests per day.Remember to start simple and iterate. Measure performance continuously. Adjust configurations based on actual load. With these practices, gRPC can be a reliable backbone for your microservices architecture.
I hope this guide helps you build robust systems. If you have questions, feel free to reach out. Happy coding! 📘 , , , and to the channel! is an AI-driven publishing company co-founded by author . By leveraging advanced AI technology, we keep our publishing costs incredibly low—some books are priced as low as —making quality knowledge accessible to everyone.Stay tuned for updates and exciting news. When shopping for books, search for to find more of our titles. Use the provided link to enjoy !Be sure to check out our creations:

item.title: gopay is now redbranded as Conductor
item.author: Irfan
item.content:encoded: I’ve been building a payment switch for the past year, which I initially named GoPay. I recently discovered a trademark conflict with another company using the same name for their payment tool, so I’ve rebranded the system to Conductor to avoid any potential trademark issues.

item.title: Create a Text Editor in Go - A Text Editor
item.author: Andres Court
item.content:encoded:
You can access the code of this chapter in the Kilo-Go github repository in the branch.Currently your file structure should look something like this:
Insert ordinary characters First we want to be able to insert new characters, and if needed to be able to create a new lineFile: editor/operations.go
Prevent inserting special characters Now that we can insert characters, if we try to insert or the editor does not understand them and we want to disable them for now doesn't have a backslash-escape representation like (, , etc) but we know it is represented by the byte key is represented by the escape sequenceFor the time being we will not be doing anything when the user press the key, but we just want to disable it so no side effects occur if the user press it. Also by the way we've wrote the method, we will disable any function keys being pressedNow that we can make changes to our files, it is time to be able to save them to diskNote: if an error occurs while saving the file, we will not exit the application, we will show the error to the user and log itWe want to keep track if the file we are showing the user differs from the one saved, so we can alert them if for example they want to quit without saving the fileNow that we control the flag, we will want to let the user know that they are trying to quit without saving the fileLets implement the and keysFile: editor/operations.go
Backspacing at the start of a line Currently, doesn't do anything when the cursor is at the beginning of a line. When the user backspaces at the beginning of a line, we want to append the contents of that line to the previous line, and then delete the current lineFile: editor/operations.goThe last keyboard input operation we will be going through is the implementation of the key, which allows the user to insert new lines into the text, or split a line into two lines.File: editor/operations.goWe want know to have the ability to prompt the user for input, so when they want to create a new file we can ask them for its name We've added the key map to test the promptNow that we can prompt things to the user, let's implement the functionality, and it will only work at the moment when we run our editor without arguments

item.title: Go Unit Testing: Structure & Best Practices
item.author: Rost
item.content:encoded:
Go's built-in testing package provides a powerful, minimalist framework for writing unit tests without external dependencies. Here are the testing fundamentals, project structure, and advanced patterns to build reliable Go applications.
Why Testing Matters in Go Go's philosophy emphasizes simplicity and reliability. The standard library includes the package, making unit testing a first-class citizen in the Go ecosystem. Well-tested Go code improves maintainability, catches bugs early, and provides documentation through examples. If you're new to Go, check out our Go Cheat Sheet for a quick reference of the language fundamentals.Key benefits of Go testing:: No external frameworks required: Concurrent test execution by default: Minimal boilerplate code: Coverage reports, benchmarks, and profiling: Easy integration with automated pipelines
Project Structure for Go Tests Go tests live alongside your production code with a clear naming convention:myproject/ ├── go.mod ├── main.go ├── calculator.go ├── calculator_test.go ├── utils/ │ ├── helper.go │ └── helper_test.go └── models/ ├── user.go └── user_test.go Test files end with Tests are in the same package as the code (or use suffix for black-box testing)Each source file can have a corresponding test file
Package Testing Approaches (same package): (external package):Every test function follows this pattern: / : Mark test as failed but continue / : Mark test as failed and stop immediately / : Log output (only shown with flag) / : Skip the test: Run test in parallel with other parallel tests
Table-Driven Tests: The Go Way Table-driven tests are the idiomatic Go approach for testing multiple scenarios. With Go generics, you can also create type-safe test helpers that work across different data types:Single test function for multiple scenariosEasy to add new test casesClear documentation of expected behaviorBetter test organization and maintainability go go go ./...
go TestAdd
go TestCalculate/addition
go 4
go 30s
go go coverage.out
go tool cover coverage.out
go tool cover coverage.out
go count coverage.out : Run tests marked with checks: Enable race detector (finds concurrent access issues): Specify GOMAXPROCS values: Run each test n times: Stop on first test failure
Test Helpers and Setup/Teardown Mark helper functions with to improve error reporting:
Mocking and Dependency Injection When testing code that interacts with databases, using interfaces makes it easy to create mock implementations. If you're working with PostgreSQL in Go, see our comparison of Go ORMs for choosing the right database library with good testability.
Popular Testing Libraries The most popular Go testing library for assertions and mocks:: Google's mocking framework with code generation: Standard library for testing HTTP handlers: Integration testing with Docker containers: BDD-style testing frameworkWhen testing integrations with external services like AI models, you'll need to mock or stub those dependencies. For example, if you're using Ollama in Go, consider creating interface wrappers to make your code more testable.Go includes built-in support for benchmarks:Output shows iterations per second and memory allocations.: Use the slice of structs pattern for multiple test cases: Better organization and can run subtests selectivelyTest exported functions first: Focus on public API behavior: Each test should verify one thingUse meaningful test names: Describe what is being tested and expected outcomeDon't test implementation details: Test behavior, not internalsUse interfaces for dependencies: Makes mocking easierAim for high coverage, but quality over quantity: 100% coverage doesn't mean bug-freeRun tests with -race flag: Catch concurrency issues earlyUse TestMain for expensive setup: Avoid repeating setup in each test
Example: Complete Test Suite Go's testing framework provides everything needed for comprehensive unit testing with minimal setup. By following Go idioms like table-driven tests, using interfaces for mocking, and leveraging built-in tools, you can create maintainable, reliable test suites that grow with your codebase.These testing practices apply to all types of Go applications, from web services to CLI applications built with Cobra & Viper. Testing command-line tools requires similar patterns with additional focus on testing input/output and flag parsing.Start with simple tests, gradually add coverage, and remember that testing is an investment in code quality and developer confidence. The Go community's emphasis on testing makes it easier to maintain projects long-term and collaborate effectively with team members.

item.title: 🎮 Understanding Linux Cgroups
item.author: Ajinkya Singh
item.content:encoded:
🚀 I'm Building My Own Container Runtime!This is part of a complete series where I'm building - a container runtime from scratch. Check it out on GitHub!I'm sharing everything I learn while building my own container runtimeMost concepts come from videos, documentation, and LLM-assisted learning (for educational purposes)Focus: Understanding through practice - raw Linux commands and practical implementation: When building your own container, DON'T copy code from sources - it kills the fun! Write it yourself, break things, debug, and learn.Deep understanding of how containers really workMaster low-level Linux conceptsLearn by doing, not just readingIt's incredibly fun when things finally click! are a Linux kernel feature that allows you to allocate, limit, and monitor system resources for processes and groups of processes.Cgroups = Resource Control + Resource Isolation + Resource Accounting Imagine you're running a web server that hosts multiple applications:: Needs 8GB RAM, 40% CPU: Needs 4GB RAM, 30% CPU : Needs 2GB RAM, 20% CPUWithout cgroups, these applications could:❌ Steal resources from each other❌ Create unpredictable performance✅ Guarantee each application gets its resources✅ Prevent any single app from hogging the system✅ Maintain stable, predictable performanceThink of a with limited parking spaces:
Without Cgroups (No Rules) Early shoppers take all parking spacesLate arrivals have nowhere to park
With Cgroups (Managed Parking) : 40 spaces reserved: 30 spaces reserved: 30 spaces reservedEach group is guaranteed their allocation, and no group can exceed their limit.1. Define Resource Groups └─> Create named groups for each resource type
2. Set Resource Limits └─> Assign specific limits to each group
3. Launch Applications └─> Start apps within their assigned groups
4. Kernel Enforces Limits └─> Automatic enforcement, no manual intervention needed All cgroups live in the filesystem at :/sys/fs/cgroup/ ├── memory/ # RAM control ├── cpu/ # CPU time control ├── cpuset/ # CPU core assignment ├── blkio/ # Disk I/O control ├── net_cls/ # Network control └── devices/ # Device access control Out-of-memory (OOM) handlingCPU percentage allocationCPU core assignment (pinning)Network class assignmentsIOPS (operations per second)
Creating a Memory Control Group : You want to run a data processing script that shouldn't use more than 20MB of RAM.
Step 1: Create the Control Group
cgcreate memory:data_processor
cgset memory.limit_in_bytes20971520 data_processor
cgset memory.memsw.limit_in_bytes20971520 data_processor
Step 3: Run Your Application
cgexec memory:data_processor python3 process_data.py
What Happens When Memory Limit Is Exceeded? Application starts ↓ Allocates memory ↓ Reaches 20MB limit ↓ Tries to allocate more ↓ ┌─────────────────┐ │ Kernel detects │ │ violation │ └────────┬────────┘ ↓ ┌─────────────────┐ │ OOM Killer │ │ Activated │ └────────┬────────┘ ↓ Process killed /sys/fs/cgroup/memory/data_processor/memory.usage_in_bytes
/sys/fs/cgroup/memory/data_processor/memory.oom_control There are to control CPU usage:
Method 1: Hard Limits (CFS Quota) Use this when you need that are always enforced.CPU Limit % = (cfs_quota_us / cfs_period_us) × 100 : Time window (default: 100,000 μs = 100ms): CPU time allowed in that window
Example: Limit to 35% CPU
cgcreate cpu:video_encoder
cgset cpu.cfs_quota_us35000 video_encoder
cgexec cpu:video_encoder ffmpeg input.mp4 output.mp4
Method 2: Soft Limits (CPU Shares) Use this when you want during resource contention.Maximum value: (represents 100%)Only enforced when multiple processes compete for CPUIf CPU is idle, processes can use more than their shareProcess CPU % = (process_shares / total_shares) × 100
Example: Three Applications Sharing CPU : You have three services running:: Should get 50% during contention: Should get 30% during contention: Should get 20% during contention
cgcreate cpu:web_server cgcreate cpu:background_jobs cgcreate cpu:monitoring
cgset cpu.shares512 web_server cgset cpu.shares307 background_jobs cgset cpu.shares205 monitoring cgexec cpu:web_server nginx cgexec cpu:background_jobs python worker.py cgexec cpu:monitoring ./monitor.sh
Hard Limits vs Soft Limits CPU Available: 100% Process usage: 30% ████████ Unused: 70% (wasted even if CPU is idle) Soft Limit (30% shares, no contention):CPU Available: 100% Process usage: 100% ████████████████████████████████ (Can use full CPU when alone) Soft Limit (30% shares, with contention):CPU Available: 100% Process 1: 30% ████████ Process 2: 40% ███████████ Process 3: 30% ████████ (Shares enforced when competing)
Example 1: Development Environment : You're running Docker containers for development:: 2GB RAM, 40% CPU: 1GB RAM, 30% CPU: 512MB RAM, 20% CPU
cgcreate memory,cpu:dev_database cgset memory.limit_in_bytes2147483648 dev_database cgset cpu.cfs_quota_us40000 dev_database
cgcreate memory,cpu:dev_api cgset memory.limit_in_bytes1073741824 dev_api cgset cpu.cfs_quota_us30000 dev_api
cgcreate memory,cpu:dev_redis cgset memory.limit_in_bytes536870912 dev_redis cgset cpu.cfs_quota_us20000 dev_redis
Example 2: Machine Learning Training : Training ML model that needs lots of resources but shouldn't crash the system. cgcreate memory,cpu:ml_training cgset memory.limit_in_bytes16106127360 ml_training cgset cpu.cfs_quota_us300000 ml_training cgexec memory,cpu:ml_training python train_model.py
Example 3: CI/CD Pipeline : Running automated tests that shouldn't hog server resources. cgcreate memory,cpu:ci_runner cgset memory.limit_in_bytes4294967296 ci_runner cgset cpu.shares256 ci_runner cgexec memory,cpu:ci_runner npm
Example 4: Multi-Tenant Web Hosting : Hosting multiple customer websites on one server. cgcreate memory,cpu:customer_a cgset memory.limit_in_bytes8589934592 customer_a cgset cpu.shares768 customer_a cgcreate memory,cpu:customer_b cgset memory.limit_in_bytes4294967296 customer_b cgset cpu.shares256 customer_b

item.title: 🧠 Detecting and Preventing Goroutine Leaks in Production (Leak Detection in Go)
item.author: Serif COLAKEL
item.content:encoded:
Goroutines are one of Go’s biggest superpowers — lightweight, fast, and easy to spin up. But with great power comes great responsibility. Left unchecked, goroutines can , grow in number over time, consume memory, and eventually bring down your service.Goroutine leaks are sneaky. They often don’t break your code immediately… but they slowly eat your system alive.In this guide, we’ll explore:What causes goroutine leaksReal-world patterns that accidentally leakHow to debug them (pprof, trace, runtime APIs)How to prevent leaks using context cancellation and proper channel patternsProduction-ready best practicesA goroutine leak happens when a goroutine , usually because it is:waiting on a select case that never fireswaiting for a context that is never cancelledpart of a consumer/producer pipeline with no exit conditionOver time, the number of goroutines grows:If this spikes over hours/days, you probably have a leak.Here’s a typical bug many developers run into: blocks forever on The goroutine never exitsNow imagine this in a loop spawning workers every request. That’s how production incidents happen.This is one of the of leaks in production.Problem: If the context is cancelled, the goroutine is . It keeps sleeping, then tries to send into where nobody is listening → goroutine leak.Use context to tell goroutines when to stop:Now the goroutine exits gracefully when the context is cancelled.Under load: 100 req/sec → 100 goroutines/sec → → goodbye memory.Fix: use a worker pool (bounded concurrency).Pipeline pattern gone wrong:Problem: channel never closes → consumer blocks forever.Track this metric in Prometheus:If it constantly grows → 🔥 leak.
2. pprof (Goroutine Profiles) go tool pprof http://localhost:6060/debug/pprof/goroutine goroutines stuck on same stack repeated thousands of timesTrace gives a timeline of goroutine lifecycle:go trace.out go tool trace trace.out You'll see goroutines that:Leaked goroutines capture memory:If heap grows in parallel with goroutines → confirmed leak.
1. Always use context with goroutines
2. Producer closes, not consumer:
3. Avoid unbounded goroutine creation
4. Use with defaults to avoid deadlocks
5. Timeouts for every external callNever trust remote systems:
6. Monitor goroutine count aggressivelyif go_goroutines > 2000 (and keeps increasing) alert: "Possible goroutine leak" ✅ Do all goroutines have a way to exit?✅ Are contexts cancelled?✅ Are channels closed when done?✅ Is concurrency bounded?✅ Are there blocking operations inside goroutines?✅ Do we monitor goroutine count?Goroutine leaks are one of the most common hidden problems in Go microservices — especially under real production load.By understanding how leaks happen and using proper patterns (context cancellation, closing channels, bounded concurrency), you can:keep your services reliable and fastconfidently observe and debug concurrency issuesHappy debugging & happy coding! 🔥🐹
